sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing.
He describes a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, particularly with solid foods, and occasionally experiences some pain.
He reports that liquids do not cause this issue.
He has not experienced any coughing, vomiting, or blood in his stool.
He denies any weight loss or chest pain.
He has a past medical history of diabetes and high blood pressure, for which he is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day and norvasc.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 120/50 mmHg.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling or masses.
The thyroid feels normal, and there are no lymph nodes.
The throat exam is normal, with no masses or swelling.
The lung exam is clear, and the heart exam is normal with no murmurs.
The belly exam reveals epigastric tenderness, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity exam is fine.
The neurological exam is normal.
Test Results:
None mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has difficulty swallowing, likely due to a narrowing of the esophagus, which may be a stricture.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing; narrowing of the esophagus; diabetes; high blood pressure.
Plan:
I prescribed prilosec 20mg once a day for reflux.
I recommended that the patient chew foods frequently and thoroughly, especially hard foods like steak or meat, to prevent them from getting stuck.
I referred the patient to gastroenterology for a scope to investigate the narrowing of the esophagus.
I ordered a repeat hemoglobin A1c, kidney function blood work, and an eye referral to ophthalmology to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I will continue the patient on metformin and norvasc.
I will see the patient back in a month to review the results of the blood work and make any necessary adjustments.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient back in a month to review the results of the blood work and make any necessary adjustments.
I will also check on the patient's progress with the prilosec and the referral to gastroenterology.
